data.title: POET Technologies: Potential Remains, But So Do The Risks (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

POET Technologies (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:POET), a Canadian firm developing photonic integrated circuits and optical engines, is working to reshape how optical data moves across systems. Its business centers on the Optical Interposer platform, which is designed to combine photonics and electronics on a single chip. The goal is faster, more energy-efficient connectivity, mainly for AI, cloud infrastructure, and large-scale data centers.

Back in September, I rated POET Technologies a Buy. The call was based on a few key factors, mainly its partnership with Mitsubishi Electric and what looked like solid momentum in the 800G and 1.6T optical engine markets. At first, the market agreed. POET increased +50% by the end of the year. But that run didn’t last. The stock has since pulled back and now trades about 4% below where I first got in. So the original thesis hasn’t been invalidated, but it’s definitely facing a tougher test now.

This week’s Q1 2025 earnings report places that original investment thesis in sharper relief. With early product shipments now in motion, a purpose-built high-volume facility operational in Malaysia, and a strong industry reception at OFC 2025, POET appears to be transitioning from blueprint to execution. My view is that POET is now entering a defining stretch in which its long-touted promise must begin to materialize as repeatable revenue and operational leverage.

POET’s Revenue Growth

POET Technologies’ Q1 2025 revenues increased from a barely-there $8,710 a year ago to $166,760 this quarter, a 1,813.74% surge. It marks the first signs of actual monetization from non-recurring engineering services and early product shipments, particularly in the 400G and 800G transceiver segments, exactly the areas where AI infrastructure and hyperscale data centers are pouring capital.

The company also posted $0.08 in earnings per share, beating consensus by $0.14 and reversing a loss of $0.13 from the same quarter last year, not to mention the gaping $0.50 per-share loss from just one quarter ago. However, this sudden profitability was largely driven by a $15.4 million non-cash gain from the revaluation of derivative warrant liabilities. This is a sharp contrast to the $12.4 million loss on the same line item in the previous quarter and a $630,000 loss in the same period last year. This type of gain doesn’t mean the company itself is suddenly more profitable in the usual sense. But even so, it makes the earnings look much better on paper, which can change how investors see the company and reinforces POET's ability to leverage financial structuring as it scales.

These earnings were further buoyed by a significant increase in interest and other income, rising tenfold from $52,000 in Q1 2024 to $528,000 this quarter, underscoring improved treasury management and a stronger cash position heading into a critical commercial ramp-up phase.

POET’s Product Shipments Begin

The numbers, while modest, have laid the groundwork. But what matters more is that POET finally shipped final design samples of its Infinity line—400G FR4, 800G 2xFR4, and 800G DR8—to three major tech customers. These are late-stage validation shipments, and they came out of the company’s high-volume facility in Malaysia, which signal that the company is moving from prototype to production in markets that demand reliability at scale.

At the 2025 Optical Fiber Communications Conference, POET debuted of its POET Teralight engines and the Blazar external light. Blazar, in particular, is aimed at a long-standing cost bottleneck in AI architecture: chip-to-chip optical links. Hyperscale data centers are already drowning in latency and overhead as they try to scale up bandwidth for AI workloads. If POET’s tech really can shift that equation, it matters a lot.

But maybe even more important than the technology is the structure behind it. POET is trying to control the ecosystem they come from. It finalized a 10,000-square-foot wafer-level cleanroom in Malaysia and fully acquired Super Photonics Xiamen [SPX]. Together, those form the backbone of a “China Plus One” strategy, which is about minimizing geopolitical exposure while tightening the feedback loop between design, testing, and production. So instead of sending pieces of the process to third parties and hoping it all syncs, POET is building an integrated line it controls, an unusual level of verticality in a microcap hardware name.

Which brings me to the $25 million equity raise. The round was done at a 21.8% premium to market, had no middlemen extracting fees, and was filled by a single institutional investor. I think this configuration could be viewed as a clear signal of conviction. The fact that this capital is earmarked for general growth and working capital, not debt repayment or survival runway, positions the raise as proactive and opportunistic, not reactive.

POET’s Valuation

If you were throwing money at speculative plays like POET and anything with an AI label last fall, you probably remember the mood was a lot looser. Risk was in, everyone was seeing upside. But then a Trump 2.0 administration kicked off a fresh round of confusion, complete with market shocks tied to a reignited tariff war. Appetite turned to indigestion pretty fast. So right now, in this new context, there’s a particular kind of tension that defines POET Technologies: a company with almost no visible earnings track record and barely any revenue to speak of, somehow commanding a $350 million market cap and a share price hovering at $4.48.

On paper, it shouldn’t work. The first P/E estimate doesn’t show up until 2027, at a speculative 19.48x. That’s because, as these speculative, high-risk plays go, POET is trading on story. But in fairness, the story has legs.

Analysts tracking the company are projecting an annual rate of return of 31.99%, thanks to a forecasted revenue explosion of 302% year-over-year.

For context, the sector median sits at just 6.78%, which puts POET’s supposed trajectory over 4,300% above industry norms.

But revenue actually declined 32.07% year-over-year prior to this. So the promise of triple-digit growth has to leap over the fact that, until very recently, the business was shrinking.

This gap between potential and performance came into full view in the company’s Q1 2025 earnings. On the surface, things looked great: a net profit of $6.3 million and a headline shift into the black. But that profit came almost entirely from that non-cash fair value gain on warrant liabilities, a technical accounting swing, not real economic output. Strip that away, and the company is still losing money.

More telling is the revenue figure. For the quarter, POET posted just $166,670 in sales, falling short of analyst expectations by $232,380. So even as the company gains visibility through tech demos, new product lines, and expanded manufacturing capacity, it hasn’t shown the ability to convert any of that into consistent, scalable revenue.

Weak Revenue Base

There’s a kind of suspended reality surrounding POET Technologies right now, the kind where the machinery of future success is being loudly assembled even as the present business model fails to support it. This is a company staging for scale, ramping CapEx, building out working capital, pushing out new tech, but still struggling to show that anyone’s actually buying what it’s selling.

Start with the top line. Let's go back to the fact that a large chunk of what POET books comes from non-recurring engineering contracts. These are one-off custom deals, not durable product sales. So while they help create the illusion of commercial activity, they don’t build a customer base or a revenue stream that can compound over time. That distinction matters, because without repeat business or widespread adoption, you’re just freelancing.

Going over the financials, company’s cash flow profile deepens my concern. Operating cash burn in Q1 2025 reached $8.9 million, worsening from $4.6 million the year before and even slightly exceeding the $8.7 million burned in Q4 2024.

On a trailing twelve-month basis, POET has torched $27.7 million, up from $23.3 million a year prior. It’s accelerating in the wrong direction.

To keep the engine running, POET has defaulted to one option: equity issuance. In the TTM, more than $80 million has been raised by issuing stock, which has kept operations afloat but steadily diluted existing shareholders. In effect, the company is paying its bills by cutting the same pie into smaller and smaller slices. That model works for a while, but only if there’s a clear path to organic revenue, and that path still isn’t visible.

Yet, the balance sheet still has some breath in it. POET holds $48.64 million in cash and carries just $7.32 million in debt, giving it a net cash position and some protection from credit market swings.

This cushion provides room to maneuver, but it’s also misleading. Because unless revenue catches up [see above], the company will inevitably have to go back to the market to raise more. And every time it does, the ownership math gets worse for current investors.

Meanwhile, capital expenditures are sending a very different signal. Over the last twelve months, POET spent $7.3 million on CapEx, a notable increase over past norms.

Working capital has exploded 796% year-over-year. In isolation, these figures would suggest a company gearing up for real scale, confident that large orders and recurring revenue are just around the corner. But when stacked against very weak revenues, non-recurring sales, and worsening cash burn, the investments feel more speculative than strategic.

Rising R&D Burden

The cost structure also reflects the turbulence of transition. In Q1 2025, R&D spending spiked to $4.3 million, more than double what the company spent a year ago and up sharply from $3.4 million just last quarter. On one level, that’s par for the course in high-tech ventures. Even so, the size and inconsistency of the increase point to a company having a hard time balancing its product development efforts with the push to get those products to market. And it’s not limited to R&D. The quarter also included $0.8 million in stock-based compensation and $0.7 million in depreciation, which added to expenses without doing anything to help cash flow.

Meanwhile, POET’s acquisition of the remaining 24.8% of Super Photonics Xiamen [SPX] is clearly central to POET’s commercial roadmap, so consolidating full control makes strategic sense. But the deal carried a $6.85 million non-cash loss, a hit that reflects the cost of ambition and, more crucially, the risks baked into POET’s capital allocation strategy. My concern is that it’s one thing to invest in vertical integration when revenues are scaling. It’s another when sales are still limping in from irregular engineering contracts.

Despite everything, the company still carries an “A” Growth Grade. But without any consensus analyst coverage on core profitability metrics, no EBITDA, EBIT, or EPS forecasts, there’s no critical wall pushing back on that narrative which makes it a lot easier to believe the projections when no one is checking the math. All of this funnels toward a projected share price of $9.29 which implies over 100% upside from where it trades today [see the FAST Graphs "Forecasting" data above].

Final Verdict

POET Technologies gets a Hold from me, down from a Buy. The tech is interesting, the long-term pitch still makes sense, and the strategy isn’t without merit. But there’s still no real product revenue to speak of, the cash burn hasn’t slowed, and most of the recent gains came from accounting maneuvers, not operations. Until the company starts delivering results that aren’t propped up by financial smoke, I’m on the sidelines.

data.title: Tech Is Crushing Dividends - And I Love It
data.description:
Over the past several years, technology stocks have been taking the market by storm. In sharp contrast, non-tech dividend stocks like those found in the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD), as well as higher-yielding sectors like REITs (VNQ) and utilities (XLU), have trailed significantly:

While earlier this year there appeared to be a major market reversal, with dividend stocks taking the lead over tech while the Nasdaq (QQQ) experienced a sharp pullback, over the past several weeks tech seems to have renewed its rapid growth and is now leaving dividend stocks in the dust once again.

As most of my regular readers know, I invest primarily in dividend stocks. As a result, you may think that I am discouraged by this turn of events—and possibly even reconsidering my investing strategy. However, in this article, I will detail why I could not be happier that tech stocks are crushing dividend stocks right now.

Why Tech Stocks Are Crushing Dividend Stocks

The biggest reason why tech stocks have crushed dividend stocks over the past several years is that artificial intelligence has emerged as a revolutionary technology that is opening up enormous growth potential for mega-cap technology stocks—whether it be NVIDIA (NVDA) as the leading supplier of AI-enabling semiconductors, or Microsoft (MSFT) with its aggressive investments in AI products and researchers, including into OpenAI, or even Meta Platforms (META), Alphabet (GOOGL)(GOOG), Apple (AAPL), and Amazon (AMZN), which are also making very aggressive bets on AI technologies and AI-enabled products and applications. Even car companies like Tesla (TSLA) are emerging as AI behemoths due to their rapid advances in self-driving technology and aggressive investments in autonomous robots that should have enormous value to consumers and corporations. As such, it is evident that the tech space is booming due to AI's ascendancy and apparent readiness for prime time.

Meanwhile, dividend stocks have been weighed down by long-term interest rates being higher for longer, as well as uncertainties about trade policy. While this trade uncertainty certainly slammed tech stocks as well earlier this year, it also hurts long-dividend-paying stocks due to the fact that many of them are multinational in nature and also, in some cases, are very involved in the energy sector (XLE)—which was significantly hurt by the potential for tariffs and a corresponding economic downturn.

Today, however, the threat of a major economic disruption from tariffs has subsided substantially due to a thawing of tensions between the U.S. and China—which are the world's two largest economies—as well as a deal between the United States and the United Kingdom and the potential for many more major trade agreements in the coming days and weeks. That said, long-term interest rates remain relatively high, and the Fed is showing no signs of wanting to cut interest rates in the near future due to sustained economic performance in the United States and continued fears that tariffs may cause inflation to reaccelerate. This is weighing on dividend stocks in general because they tend to be much more interest rate-sensitive than tech stocks. The yield generated by dividend stocks typically competes with bond yields as an income source for income-focused investors, leading to a material headwind for dividend stocks whenever bond yields rise.

There are additionally many dividend-paying stocks—especially real asset ones like REITs, utilities, infrastructure, and midstream companies—that all use a good bit of leverage. Therefore, when interest rates are higher, their cost of capital is also higher. Meanwhile, most mega-tech stocks are cash-rich, have little debt on their balance sheets, and do not pay much in the way of dividends, so none of these headwinds affect them nearly to the same degree that they impact dividend stocks.

Why I Am So Happy About It

So with all that bad news for dividend stocks, why am I so happy that tech stocks are crushing dividend stocks—especially given that my dividend stock exposure significantly outweighs my tech stock exposure? Well, the answer is simply that I plan to continue being a net buyer of dividend stocks relative to tech stocks—and certainly relative to holding cash—over time. Therefore, I am very excited that I continue to have golden opportunities to buy high-quality, high-yield stocks with strong balance sheets and underlying fundamentals at very attractive valuations.

Sure, it feels good in the short term to see a lot of green in my portfolio and make a lot of money. To be clear, I have been able to outperform the S&P 500 by a significant margin over the course of my investing career strictly through investing in dividend stocks. However, my future returns depend a great deal on the price at which I am able to allocate fresh capital today. And since dividend stocks by and large remain very attractively valued compared to tech stocks, this is great news for dividend investors like me—because it means we are able to buy additional shares at much more compelling valuations than tech stock investors are today.

In fact, even if I were not an individual stock picker, I still would be much more inclined to invest in dividend stocks than in tech stocks. While mega-cap-dominated indexes like the S&P 500 trade at nosebleed valuation levels based on multiple valuation models—such as the Buffett Indicator, the S&P mean reversion model, and the S&P 500 price-to-earnings model—prominent dividend growth stock ETFs like SCHD trade at very attractive dividend yields relative to their recent history, while also being filled with high-quality blue-chip names that have powered it to an 11% dividend CAGR over the past decade.

Additionally, in the individual dividend stock universe, there are many attractive opportunities, including the alternative asset management space, where you can combine growth and yield with stocks like Brookfield Asset Management (BAM) and Blue Owl Capital (OWL), trading at very reasonable valuations and combining dividend yields that significantly beat what is offered by the broader market today—along with strong high-teens expected dividend per share growth for the foreseeable future.

You can also invest in the infrastructure space with many attractive opportunities across the midstream (AMLP) and other AI-related infrastructure segments, including the likes of Energy Transfer (ET) and Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP)(BIPC). You also can buy a lot of quality REITs at deep discounts to their intrinsic value, as evidenced by the private market value of their assets. An example of this is Realty Income (O), as per my recent analysis here. You can also buy quality infrastructure funds like the Reaves Utility Income Fund (UTG) at a very reasonable price-to-earnings multiple and a 7% dividend yield, along with a stellar track record and prudent leverage.

Investor Takeaway

Tech stocks are hot right now—and have been for the past several years—and I certainly do not think without reason either, given that AI does have enormous potential to radically transform the economy. However, as a dividend investor, I could not be happier with the recent underperformance of the dividend stock space. This is because I plan to buy a lot more dividend stocks in the coming years, and I think the valuations being offered today are extremely compelling, especially relative to the broader marketplace.

Additionally, even in recent years, if I were simply a retiree, I would still be happy about the underperformance because I would be living off my dividends. I would not have to care one way or the other about the underlying stock price performance as long as my dividends continue to roll in and grow at a pace that beats inflation over time. This would especially be the case if my portfolio generated extra dividends above and beyond my living expenses—because then I could grow my passive income stream even faster than I would have been able to otherwise by reinvesting at higher yields.

As a result, while tech stock investors may be rejoicing today at their outsized returns, we are equally happy at the attractive opportunities Mr. Market is giving us in the high-yield space today at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Arbor Realty: Dividend Unlikely To Recover After 2025 As Multifamily Outlook Worsens
data.description:
In January, I published a bearish outlook on the multifamily and commercial property lender Arbor Realty Trust (NYSE:ABR) in "Arbor Realty: Short Opportunity As Southern U.S. Housing Glut Forms." My view was that high interest rates combined with signs that the housing shortage is ending would eventually cause lower rents, pushing delinquency risk higher. Since then, ABR has declined by about 23% while the S&P 500 is roughly unchanged.

Arbor declined sharply in February after its Q4 earnings missed estimates, with a staggering 20% year-over-year decline in sales. Its Q1 earnings were slightly above estimates but failed to improve sentiment due to a notable decline in loan originations and net interest income. Since then, the company's manager has noted increased focus on reducing leverage costs to offset rising delinquencies; however, its core business model remains generally unchanged, and many investors and analysts remain concerned about continued dividend cuts.

Arbor's 2025 EPS estimate has declined to $0.81, with targets ranging from $0.57 to $1.32, giving the company a forward price-to-earnings ratio of about 13X. That also means its reduced dividend ($1.2 annualized) is still likely not covered. Based on the company's guidance and analyst estimates, most expect Arbor's prospects to begin recovering in 2026.

Its recovery depends primarily on what happens next in its primary operating market. Arbor largely depends on the high-risk bridge loan market with higher LTVs that may not be easily refinanced to agency-backed CMBS loans, given bearish commercial property market conditions. Given its high leverage, a continued increase in non-performing loans can theoretically compromise its remaining book value or pose challenges in selling or improving foreclosed properties.

Arbor cannot Reduce Leverage Fast Enough

Over the past five years, Arbor has tried to reduce its effective leverage. Around 2022, when rising rates began to pressure the firm, it had total assets of nearly 11X its tangible book value. Today, that figure has declined to 6.9X, which is still notable, offset by more than doubling its expected loan loss rate. See below:

Although this rate has not increased markedly over the past year, it failed to decline as many had hoped. That said, its overall balance of non-performing loans exceeding 60 days past due fell to $511M in Q1 from $651M in Q1 2024. That said, its total unpaid principal balance on loans with modifications was a staggering $949M at the end of Q1, representing ~8.3% of its total structured business loans. In most of these loans, Arbor required borrowers to invest additional capital to provide rate relief.

Arbor has also seen a shocking buildup in Real Estate-Owned "REO," which is now a net total of $302M, up from $176M in Q4 2024. This is from nine multifamily properties, two office buildings, and two land parcels after seven Q1 foreclosures. In general, foreclosures do not provide sufficient capital to offset loans because these properties are likely foreclosed on because their value is falling, in addition to selling costs, rehab, and property management challenges.

A slow decline in multifamily lending stability threatens Arbor's core business model. We're seeing high levels of loan modifications as it looks to keep its delinquency rate from rising. While that limits its direct risk, its ability to refinance these loans into agency-backed CMBS originations is becoming compromised. In 2023, I specifically detailed this issue regarding its "Agency Takeout" Model. The company makes money by refinancing high-risk bridge loans into lower-risk agency CMBS. However, that is not possible if LTVs on its bridge loans rise due to the combination of lower property values, higher interest rates, and modified loans.

Multifamily Property Outlook Worsens

Arbor Realty is doing what it can to manage this strain, mainly through aggressively lowering its leverage. In my view, its decision to reduce its dividend is also a wise preservation of capital. That said, most of its assets are concentrated in sunbelt multifamily properties, which are generally riskier, given that around 96% are bridge loans.

In its last investor call, managers saw 2025 as a "transition year" with a revised dividend and a buildup in REO inventories. That said, there is limited evidence to suggest that fundamental negative economic headwinds will fade by the end of the year. Yes, the market is not looking for a rise or decline in the SOFR rate (set by the Fed), which has been the primary factor weighing on the market, given commercial mortgage rates are around 7-8% today. Overall, US commercial property prices fell as rates rose, rebounded, and slipped again last month, potentially pointing to another leg lower.

Looking at economic fundamentals that impact Arbor, concentrated in the sunbelt state multifamily, the outlook appears worse. Since I covered Arbor last, we've seen a continued increase in the southern US rental vacancy rate and seasonal increases in home inventories:

The "housing shortage" has pushed rents higher, offsetting extremely low property capitalization rates and high borrowing costs with high NOI growth. I believe many investors and analysts expect the housing shortage to push rents up forever. However, in the South, asking rents begin to decline as the shortage transitions into a glut. Since I covered Arbor last, condo sales have dropped with slightly lower prices. Developers are pulling back construction activity, while the hiring rate continues to plummet, signaling a general economic contraction and alleviation of the housing shortage.

These data are only slightly worse than when I last covered Arbor, but they continue to point to long-term strain in the multifamily residential market. Although interest rates are unlikely to rise, the Federal Reserve is not expected to continue cutting rates beyond 25 bps.

Housing construction levels, though falling, are still relatively high compared to the 2010s norm. In my view, this data indicates likely overdevelopment of properties, particularly multifamily, that will not be met with sufficient demand. Rents are not rising while inflation is likely pushing property managers' overhead costs, harming NOIs.

It is likely true that, as CEO Ivan Kaufman mentioned, the initial occupancy issues related to COVID and post-COVID are improving. That said, I disagree with his take that the market is bottoming out overall. Although COVID rent-forbearance properties created strains, those challenges were heavily offset by skyrocketing rents and low rates around 2020-2022. The market faces likely sustained rates around 4%, while we're seeing emerging signals of lower economic demand and excessive housing development, particularly in the Southern US.

The Bottom Line

To me, the fact that Arbor is performing so poorly during a relatively decent economic period is alarming. Most economic data suggests that the challenges facing the multifamily development sector continue to mount. The best Arbor can do is likely seek to sell REO properties quickly and reduce its leverage so its book value will not suffer from what I expect to be a continued increase in non-performing loans.

My bearish thesis on Arbor remains intact, and I expect ABR to lose value. Since I last covered it, I argue that economic conditions facing the multifamily market have worsened. However, rents and home values are not clearly falling, suggesting that Arbor may have adequate time to continue improving its liquidity and solvency profile.

Though I remain bearish, I would not bet against it at its current price. If the Federal Reserve shifts its focus and cuts rates more aggressively as Trump aims for, Arbor's health could improve, which may alleviate strain for Arbor's borrowers. Still, I see limited evidence for a return to pre-2022 interest rates, and considerable evidence that the multifamily residential property market will be comparatively slow for the next ten years compared to the past ten.

data.title: Cronos Reports Improved Financials For Q1-2025 As Demand Outpaces Supply
data.description:
Cronos (NASDAQ:CRON) reported increasing revenues and net income for Q1-2025. Its international and domestic sales have increased. The company’s stock is up 25% over the last month. It is up 6.5% since I covered it last March on Seeking Alpha. While the company boasts top market share in Israel, there are some new developments which incur risk. I continue my rating of a Hold for the company.

Q1-2025 Results

Cronos reported revenues of $32.3 million, a 27.7% increase YoY and a 6.6% increase QoQ. GrowCo revenues totaled $2.9 million and Cronos totaled $29.4 million. Increase in revenues was due to higher sales in Israel, internationally, and in Canada. The company brought in $9.2 million in revenues from Israel for the quarter. Gross profit was $13.7 million or 43% margin. Gross profit has increased 204% YoY and 52% QoQ.

Operating expenses have decreased YoY and QoQ. Cash and short-term investments were $838 million, a decrease QoQ due to CapEx spending. The company is expanding the GrowCo facilities with the expenditures. It is expected the CapEx will remain elevated as the company cures supply constraints. Cronos remains debt free. The company reported a net income of $6.1 million compared to a net loss of $2.2 million YoY. The company did not report free cash flow for the quarter.

Market consensus for Q2-2025 revenue is $32.33 million, representing an increase YoY and no change QoQ. The company will report next earnings in August.

Current Synergies and Risk

Cronos is a large Canadian cannabis LP with an international footprint and a large portfolio of brands. Cronos maintains the top market share of the medical cannabis market in Israel. Cronos also has cannabis operations in the UK, Germany, and Australia. The company’s Peace Naturals brand covers its international operations.

The company’s Canadian Spinach brand holds second place in terms of market share in Canada. The brand has been so popular that the company has experienced supply constraints. The company is expanding its GrowCo operations to meet the demand. The company acquired half ownership of GrowCo last quarter and invested $51 million in expansion efforts for their grow facility. Cronos buys 80% of GrowCo’s harvests.

Cronos had record sales in Israel for the quarter. In April, the Israeli government decided to impose up to 165% tariffs on Canadian cannabis. The tariffs have not been fully approved yet. The tariffs come in response to the government accusing Cronos of “dumping” its products in their market. This charge assumes that the company is lowering its prices below the fair market value of its export country. Cronos denies any truth in the allegations, but tariffs may still be imposed. The tariffs will bring some risk to the company’s international sales.

Cronos is reporting elevated revenues over the last five quarters, as well as profits. Operating expenses have decreased but more CapEx has been promised. The company and its stock price are undervalued. Its market cap ($806.28 million) is less than its total books value ($992.5 million). The company’s stock price ($2.14) trades below its book value per share ($2.77) and NAV per share value ($2.89). The stock price trades about $1 below its 52-week high ($3.12). data.title: BUI: 6.7% Yield On Defensive Utilities (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Amid all of the chaos, Utilities are having a good year so far in 2025 - they're up nearly 8%, as investors scramble for defensive positions to protect their portfolios from market volatility.

If you're interested in plugging into the Utilities sector, but don't have the time to research these companies, there are several closed-end funds, or "CEF's," that offer exposure to this sector, such as the BlackRock Utilities, Infrastructure & Power Opportunities Trust (NYSE:BUI).

Fund Profile:

BUI's

"investment objective is to provide total return through a combination of current income, current gains and long-term capital appreciation. The Trust seeks to achieve its investment objective by investing primarily in equity securities issued by companies that are engaged in the Utilities, Infrastructure, and Power Opportunities business segments anywhere in the world and by utilizing an option writing (selling) strategy in an effort to enhance current gains." (BUI site)

BUI began in 2011. It held 54 positions, as of 4/30/25, with average daily volume of 56K, and net assets of $514M. The expense ratio is 1.08%, with scant leverage involved.

Holdings:

As you'd expect, Utilities are BUI's largest sector exposure, at ~48%. Capital Goods follows, at 27.44%, with Energy at 10.48%. It has less than 5% exposure to several other sectors and sub-sectors, such as Commercial & Professional Services, Materials, and Transportation:

Its main regional exposure is the U.S., at 62%, distantly followed by France, at 9.3%, the UK, at 7.8%, Italy, at 5%, and Canada, at 3.7%. It also has exposures of less than 3% each in several other countries:

BUI's top 10 positions include Next Era Energy (NEE) the biggest market cap U.S. utility; and National Grid (NGG), comprising over 9% of BUI's portfolio.

BUI also has positions in GE Vernova (GEV), a relative newcomer, founded in 2021; SSE, formerly Southern and Scottish Energy; Trane Technologies (TT), an Irish company with worldwide operations, founded in 1871; in addition to midstream energy giant Williams Companies (WMB) and LNG firm Cheniere Energy. Multinational Enel Spa, French construction company Vinci, and Elia Group, which operates transmission systems in Belgium and Germany.

Large cap stocks dominate BUI's portfolio, at ~94%, with mid caps at 4.7%.

The U.S. dollar (DXY) is the main currency exposure, at 63.5%; followed by the Euro, at 21.9%. The U.S. $ is down ~7% vs. the Euro so far in 2025.

Dividends:

BUI pays monthly distributions - it generally goes ex-dividend in mid-month, and pays at the end of the month. At its intraday 5/16/25 price of $24.34, BUI yielded 6.7%.

Management raised the monthly payout by 12% for 2025, to $.1360 from $.1210, where it had been since 2014.

In 2024, BUI's NII declined ~17%, while its Net Realized Gains rose rose 29%, and Unrealized Appreciation fell ~57%. Net Assets decreased $32.5M in 2023 and $32.99M in 2024:

Taxes:

23.75% of 2024 distributions were characterized as Return of Capital, "ROC." ROC offers you a tax deferral benefit, but it does decrease your tax basis, which will impact your tax liability if you sell you shares.

Performance:

2020 and 2021, COVID-impacted years, were strong performance periods for BUI, as investors fled to defensive sectors. However, rising interest rates pressured BUI's NAV and market price returns in 2022. In 2023 and 2024, BUI's performance returned to positive territory.

As of 3/31/25, BUI's 5-year returns were its strongest, at 11.63% for NAV and 12.93% for Market Price. Its 10-year returns were lower, at 7.43% for NAV, and 8.70% for price.

Its 3-year returns were its lowest, due to the decline in 2022, while its 1-year 7.72% NAV return is a bit above its 10-year mark, and its 1-year 12% price return is much higher than its 10-year 8.7% figure:

Risks:

Interest Rate Risk: Rising interest rates would pressure BUI's portfolio value, as they did in 2022, when BUI's market price fell by ~17%.

Economic Risk: A recession could reduce power demand from Utilities.

Valuations:

Buying CEF's at deeper than historic discounts to NAV can be a useful strategy, due to mean reversion. CEFs' daily valuations are calculated at the end of each trading day. Not much joy here - as of the 5/15/25 close, BUI was selling at a 1.17% premium to its NAV/share, vs. its 1- and 3-year discounts of 1.23% and 1.85%, respectively, and its flat par 5-year average pricing.

Parting Thoughts:

While BlackRock Utilities, Infrastructure & Power Opportunities Trust's defensive characteristics are attractive, we prefer to wait for a discount to NAV before buying shares, rather than buying it at its current premium to NAV.

All tables furnished by Hidden Dividend Stocks Plus, unless otherwise noted.

data.title: AT&T Inc.: A Secure Income Play With Series C Preferred Shares
data.description:
AT&T Inc.'s 4.75% Depositary Shares, Series C (NYSE:T.PR.C), offer investors a fixed-income-like security supported by one of the most important telecommunications companies in the US. AT&T (T) is by itself a blue-chip telecom in the US, and its fundamentals have steadily improved over time, in particular its stable and relatively predictable free cash flows. In that sense, T.PR.C is T’s series C preferred shares, which are cumulative with optional redemption rights beginning in 2025. In my view, these preferreds look like a reasonably safe alternative for income investors even during times of economic uncertainty.

Telecom Bluechip Preferreds

AT&T Inc.'s 4.75% Series C is actually an ADS. These represent fractional interests in its 4.75% of the Perpetual Preferred Stock backed by one of the largest telecommunications companies in the US. Each depositary share corresponds to 1/1,000th of a preferred share with a $1,000 stated value. And as you might expect, a regular preferred share might be too much of a capital commitment for some investors, so that’s why the ADS under T.PR.C is useful.

The key features of this Series C preferred are a fixed annual dividend of $1.1875 per depositary share, equivalent to 4.75% of par on February 1, May 1, August 1, and November 1. Also, its dividends are cumulative. This means any unpaid dividends accrue until paid, although historically, AT&T has always made its payments on time so far.

Additionally, these preferred shares are redeemable, in whole or in part, at the option of AT&T beginning February 18, 2025. The redemption price is $25.00 per depositary share, plus any accrued and unpaid dividends. So in that sense, it’s always possible you might get your capital back earlier than you anticipate, but other than that, it’s reasonable to consider it a long-term investment. Having said that, holders of the Series C don’t have voting rights. The only scenario where they can have a vote is if AT&T misses its dividend payments for six or more quarters.

AT&T's Business Safely Supports Its Dividend

As a company, AT&T generates stable cash flows from its wireless services and fiber operations, which are the two sectors with regular revenue and high client retention. In Q1 2025, AT&T reported $30.6 billion in revenues. And in fact, they’re now in a stronger position compared to years ago, when T experienced pressure from elevated debt levels. Moreover, their free cash flow reached $3.1 billion for Q1 2025, and they’re on track for $16 billion in FCF for the full year 2025. This matters because, as a holder of the T.PR.C ADS, all you really care about is that the company has enough resources to continue supporting your dividend.

For example, AT&T’s mobile and fiber core services are growing steadily. Mobile service revenue was up 4.1% YoY, with an average revenue per user up 1.8%. Fiber segment also grew, with 261,000 net fiber adds, and revenue up 19% YoY. These are high-margin businesses that can sustain dividend payments even if the economy faces uncertainty. It’s hard to imagine that there will be a sharp decline in demand for these types of services even during a serious economic recession.

Having said that, AT&T has to first serve its debt obligations. It’s only after they’ve made all their debt and interest payments that the preferred dividends can be paid. At the bottom of the FCF seniority is the regular common stock dividends. AT&T has historically maintained a good credit rating, honoring its capital commitments even in financial stress periods.

In any event, over the years, we’ve seen AT&T’s focus shift towards its core telecommunications business. In the process, they’ve shed non-core assets and balanced their sheet while improving margins. For example, if we go back to 2020, they reported a full-year net margin of -3.62%. But if we look at their TTM Q1 2025 figures, their profit margins have already recovered markedly to 9.64%.

Similarly, their Q4 2020 debt reached $155.2 billion (excluding other regular operating liabilities). But by Q1 2025, their debt had reduced significantly to just $127.9 billion. In fact, their net debt was reduced by $1 billion in Q1 2025 alone. And interestingly, their net debt has declined by approximately $32 billion since 2020. Of course, in the process, they cut down on other non-core assets, but strategic areas like Fiber and 5G connectivity remain their priorities. After all, these are critical infrastructure services that are less sensitive to economic downturns compared to discretionary sectors.

So overall, it’s not just a healthier balance sheet, but also a safer set of cash flows because they’re now doubling down on the most resilient assets they had. In my view, all of this suggests that going forward, their revenues are very unlikely to have significant sudden contractions, which is vital for the continued support of preferred dividends regardless of the economic cycle.

Valuation And Risk Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, the Series C math is rather straightforward. First, from what I can tell, it has the same seniority as the rest of AT&T’s preferreds. So, in that sense, we can simply look at the whole amount it spends on interest and preferred dividends to gauge how safe the dividend is. We also know that there are currently 48.0 million Series A preferred shares outstanding (T.PR.A) and 70.0 million Series C shares outstanding. The Series B has been retired, so it’s no longer an issue to consider.

In total, each Series A pays a $0.3125 quarterly dividend per ADS. And the Series C pays $0.296875 quarterly per ADS. That means AT&T needs to pay roughly $35.8 million in quarterly preferred dividends. On top of that, AT&T’s latest Q1 report showed they generated $9.1 billion in cash flows from operations and spent $4.3 billion in CAPEX. And just to be extra safe, we can also consider their $1.7 billion in interest expenses for Q1 2025. In total, their CFOs seem to cover easily both the CAPEX and interest expenses, and have $3.1 left for dividends and their operations.

And by comparison, the $35.8 million required for their preferred dividends seems more than safe, which is why I believe this is probably one of the safest income plays out there besides US treasuries. Also, the Series C now trades at a $18.96 PPS, and yields $1.1875 in yearly dividends, implying a respectable preferred yield of 6.26%. This is also in line with the Series A preferreds, which trade at $19.91 and yield 6.28%.

In other words, both series are basically the same in terms of yield. And if you compare these yields to the US 10-year treasury yield of 4.42%, it actually seems you get a lot of bang for your buck. The Series C basically gives you 41.6% more yield than the risk-free rate, and in my view, the extra risk here is relatively negligible given how reliable and safe AT&T’s underlying business is. This is why I think the recent pullback in the Series C is probably a decent entry price for new investors seeking a safe and steady stream of quarterly dividends.

Having said that, during AT&T’s latest earnings call, management acknowledged some tariff concerns, especially on smartphones and network equipment. The firm expects a minor impact that can be overcome by adjusting pricing, offering longer payment plans, or higher service fees. Besides, the company considers that its supply chain is diversified enough because AT&T acquires its equipment, devices, and materials from different suppliers and countries. So, while this macro risk could become an issue, it still seems mostly insulated from the ongoing trade concerns.

Similarly, AT&T does have a share buyback program that should start in Q2 2025. AT&T is targeting up to $3 billion in repurchases by the end of 2025. Plus, the board of directors of AT&T has approved a plan to buy back up to $10 billion of the company’s shares over time. So this program could potentially divert some of AT&T’s funds towards common investors and may weaken its balance sheet a bit more than ideal for Preferred shares investors.

Likewise, I see the trading volume for these Series C preferreds is just about 114.2 thousand shares. At the current $18.6 PPS, it implies a somewhat shallow trading liquidity of $2.2 million. So, even relatively small buy or sell orders can push the price, and would probably incur noticeable slippage for larger investors. But still, since this ticker is publicly traded on the NYSE, it will always be easy to find buyers or sellers, which makes it a highly liquid preferred compared to other lesser-known preferreds not listed on exchanges or in private markets.

Conclusion: Worth A “Buy” For Income Investors

Overall, I’m impressed by how compelling these AT&T preferences are at these levels. Naturally, it’s a relatively low yield compared to other riskier pure equity plays out there. But on the flip side, these are one of the safest dividends in the market in my view. And given their healthy economic situation, management is focused on shareholder returns, and the long-term reliability of the Series C dividends appears strong for investors seeking stable income from a blue-chip issuer. Thus, I think the recent pullback on these ADS is probably a good buying opportunity for income investors who want a safe and steady stream of quarterly dividends.

data.title: Enterprise Products Partners: Time To Buy Before The Market Rebounds Further
data.description:
EPD: So Much Fear Yet Still Bottomed

Just when we thought energy stocks (including the high-quality energy infra plays) could flounder for the rest of 2025 (or potentially even through 2026… who knows?), the detente between the US and China sparked a risk-on rally that arguably took bearish investors by surprise.

The “V” bottom recovery has reclaimed most, if not all, of the losses for the Nasdaq (QQQ) (NDX) and the S&P 500 (SPX) (SP500) since the market topped out in February 2025. Even the energy sector (XLE) also marked a constructive revival in fortunes from the bottom in April, as investors lowered the threat of a global recession, while energy prices also staged their mini recovery of sorts.

As a result, we have moved past the pivotal $50 crude oil pricing zone that could see US production start to reverse its years of shale-driven gains that spurred America into the leading oil producer globally. With that, America’s leading oil & gas companies are also blessed with the byproducts that include natural gas and NGL, benefiting across the energy value chain.

For Enterprise Products Partners (NYSE:EPD), the respite couldn’t have been more keenly felt, as EPD also marked its April nadir, Dip buyers clearly saw value in EPD’s forward distribution yield of 7.6% at the recent lows, affording investors an amazing opportunity to snap up units of the leading energy infrastructure company. With a nearly 17% price recovery (not including distribution) from those lows, I assess that its 6.9% yield still looks attractively valued, even when considering that the 10Y (US10Y) still printed 4.45% as it closed the week.

While the rise in long-term yields could impose more pressure on the recovery of EPD, the resilience of the rally over the past month has assured me that high-quality midstream plays like Enterprise Products Partners are well-poised to ride the abatement of trade tensions that should boost energy flow again between the world’s top two economic superpowers, and also rest of the world.

In my previous EPD article, I reminded investors why the quality of the LP and its stable cash flows should provide much-needed sturdiness to income investors seeking a “safer” harbor to dodge the trade tensions. Yet, the fear-induced plunge spurred by Trump’s Liberation Day reminds us why there is nowhere to hide when the market goes on a selling frenzy, although high conviction investors probably viewed such market anxieties as “perfect” opportunities to snap up even more EPD units as fearful investors bailed out.

As a reminder, EPD management underscored its confidence that pricing dynamics would help to resolve the changes in production levels, urging investors to let pricing work its way through the market accordingly.

EPD: Balancing Growth And Capital Return

In addition, EPD hasn’t observed substantial revisions to its production outlook, as it maintained its $4B to $4.5B in CapEx for 2025, while pointing to a significantly reduced capital outlay of $2B to $2.5B next year. In other words, the climate remains conducive for EPD to continue pushing through its $7.6B in capital growth projects, of which $6B has been earmarked for 2025. With crude oil prices having taken back to the $60 level recently, it has dodged the critical $50 level that EPD management emphasized could lead to smaller independent producers pulling back from production, but possibly not the larger ones.

Furthermore, given EPD’s role across the energy value chain, its well-diversified (while concentrated in NGL access) strategy helps shield it from previous fears that China could also hit LPG with tariffs. However, management clarified that most of its capacity remains filled to the brim contractually, alleviating undue worries that could afflict even the top energy infra players through 2026.

In other words, unless we head into a global recession (which was never my base case, with a slowdown more likely), I have confidence that EPD’s solid financial profile, excess DCF reaching $3.6B next year, and ongoing capital allocation framework to continue driving shareholder value should mitigate selling intensity that we saw occur in April.

In addition, as the LP moves past its heightened capital spending priorities (front-loading of CapEx) in 2025, it should also offer a buffer for EPD to reallocate higher capital returns to unit holders from 2026, even if contracted capacity were to weaken post-2026. Yet, with 80% of its revenue imputed to fee-based income, I believe the investment thesis in EPD remains sound, since its valuation has also normalized through the recent lows.

Buy Or Sell EPD Units?

As seen above, EPD’s valuation has remained stable and supported whenever selling pressure attempted to hobble investors at the 9.1x forward EBITDA level. That zone is perched against the -1 standard deviation zone below its 10Y average, highlighting relative pessimism.

Yet, I don’t think a return to the heydays of above 11.2x EBITDA is reasonable in the current context, as investors navigate the long-term shift in oil and gas production in favor of green energy and lower carbon intensity prospects. Moreover, EPD’s lowered CapEx outlay from 2026 suggests that such a heightened spending environment isn’t anticipated to persist, with capital discipline and capital return as the main narrative underlining EPD’s long-term thesis.

Moreover, interest rates are expected to remain relatively high as the market digests the long-term structural changes to the US economy attributed to Trump’s fundamental restructuring. Hence, it should temper the outlook for lower rates through 2026, even as recent inflation reports have headed in the correct direction of more rate cuts over the next twelve months.

Yet, EPD’s valuation remains markedly below the highs it struck in 2025, suggesting that we should have an opportunity to enjoy a more sustained recovery through the rest of the year. However, we must be cognizant that Trump’s trade tariff pauses have a 90-day limit (including the recent hiatus with China), intimating that near-term uncertainties could still persist, lowering buying sentiments on EPD to reach levels struck earlier this year.

As seen above, EPD has climbed back up its 50-week moving average (blue line), a pivotal zone to ascertain the strength of its underlying buying sentiments. In other words, I’m increasingly certain that EPD’s uptrend bias (clearly seen) remains robust. Also, the sharp vertical drop in April hasn’t deterred investors from picking up the pieces with those highly appealing yields. Therefore, I assess that investors have remained confident in EPD’s prospects across the energy value chain through its leading role in energy infrastructure.

However, I must highlight that the $34.5 zone has seen strong selling pressure that resisted buying advances between November 2024 and March this year. Hence, investors should avoid entering too aggressively close to that zone, particularly as EPD has also reached overbought levels. For now, the bullish thesis looks set to continue, as momentum returns to energy infra plays.

Rating: Maintain Buy.

data.title: BASF: Earnings Remains Tough, Neutral Rating Confirmed
data.description:
We have historically maintained a long-biased view on BASF (OTCQX:BASFY)(OTCQX:BFFAF), but last year we prudently shifted our rating to neutral — a move that proved timely (Fig. 1). Looking ahead, we continue to see merit in maintaining an equal-weight position over a twelve-month horizon. Even after an unusually long destocking period, we believe EU chemicals volumes are bottoming out, and see much more compelling cyclical equity stories such as Arkema. Given the recent BASF development (potential IPO of the Agriculture division and intention to sell its Coatings assets), we are back to comment on the equity story.

Fig 1

Q1 Results and Our Neutral Take

BASF delivered a Q1 EBITDA of €2.62 billion (Fig. 2), which was down by 3% year-on-year. Turnover reached €17.40 billion and was also down by 1% on a comparable basis. This was due to lower price (-0.9%), a FX tailwind (0.4%), partially offset by volumes (-0.9%) and portfolio changes (+0.2%). The key to the report is the fact that net financial debt (excluding a pension deficit of €2.15 billion) stood at €20.39 billion (2.6x EBITDA). By operational MIX, upstream and downstream were down by 16% and 9%, respectively. By division, the company reported supportive results in Nutrition & Care and Chemicals, while lower results in Coatings, Battery Materials, and Agriculture Solutions. BASF recorded an outflow of almost €1.8 billion in free cash flow due to working capital requirements and lower earnings, which were also partially offset by lower CAPEX investment.

Source: BASF Q1 results ppt - Fig 2

Why are we still Neutral?

  1. Despite confirming full-year EBITDA guidance (Fig. 4), management acknowledged the current uncertainty, stating, "A reliable quantification of the impact on the global economy is not possible at this time. In light of the volatile situation, the assumptions published in the BASF Report 2024 regarding the global economic environment in 2025 remain unchanged for the time being. ” This highlights the limited visibility in the future. In number, the CEO has reiterated its Fiscal Year 2025 EBITDA range for €8-8.4 billion, with FCF estimates between €400-800 million. On the impact of tariffs, in the Q1 press release, the management commented, “Production momentum in the chemical industry and its customer industries was significantly influenced by reactions to anticipated tariff increases by the United States. Developments going forward will largely depend on the trade policy decisions made by the United States and its trading partners." We understand this stance, but this assumes a more downside risk scenario than notable improvement from current conditions. Therefore, we prefer to remain on the sidelines.

  2. Including pension, BASF's net debt is above €20 billion (Fig. 3), with a leverage ratio on the EBITDA above 2.5x. There is a viable roadmap that assumes disposal monetisation. This will likely reduce the debt by approximately €10 billion (monetisation of the coatings division and Agricultural assets); however, the disposal needs to be structured, and it is hard to anticipate the upfront proceeds for the company. That said, on a negative note, if BASF sells these assets, its cash flows and the residual portfolio MIX will have a more volatile profile.

  3. Considering its debt level and complex disposal evolution, we believe the company needs to undergo a turnaround to reduce costs. As such, we think BASF should restructure its European footprint. This will be carried with potential proceeds and will likely limit buyback capacity.

  4. With the ramp-up in China, the current chemicals spread far below the level embedded within the guide, which suggests that this project could contribute €1 billion of EBITDA. In the EU, there is a substantial oversupply environment in most commodities. Even if there were closures, the pricing and margin environment in most of the company’s commodity MIX segments will likely remain under pressure.

Fig 3

Earnings Changes and Valuation

Our last analysis reported higher leverage in the future, "at least €1 billion per year over the next three years," and was "not including BASF share buyback estimates." We continue to believe that. BASF’s large new greenfield site in China will start this year and should help earnings; however, the company highlighted that margins remain tough. EU industry overcapacity is a factor, and FX is also an incremental headwind. With an uncertainty management tone, we project BASF EBITDA at its low-end guidance of €8 billion (Fig. 4). In our previous valuation, we used a mid-cycle EBITDA projection to value the company. This is not the case, so with a €8 billion EBITDA, and the company's technical guidance on CAPEX (for D&A, we estimate almost €4 billion), interest, and a corporate tax rate of 25%, BASF adjusted net income reached €3 billion with an EPS of €3.8. This considers the current outstanding shares.

For the valuation, we applied a blended methodology with an EV/EBITDA of 8x and a P/E of 12x. Our multiple is 20% lower vs. the company's median multiple (Fig. 5) and reflects: 1) lower DPS, high earnings uncertainty, and 3) potential downside to Wall Street consensus estimates. Considering the net debt, we reached a target price of €47.2 ($13.2 in ADR). This is slightly lower than our previous target price of €49 per share.

Fig 4

Fig 5

Risks

We initiated BASF in 2022 and have long covered its downside risks. Thus, we report the following: 1) industry capacity evolution combined with supply/demand imbalances, 2) inventory cycles, and shocked from tariffs, 3) changes in commodity pricing with impact on oil and gas prices, 4) higher interest rates given BASF net debt evolution, and 5) FX changes. Additionally, we now report disposal proceeds and execution risks. BASF is also subject to lower economic activity, especially in Europe. Product litigation, CAPEX inflation, and potential environmental/safety liabilities are other risks to consider.

Conclusion

After the Q1 results, BASF offers higher downside risks with a heavy balance sheet compared to peers. We rate BASF neutral, with an expensive valuation relative to other cyclical chemical stocks within our coverage. In the long term, we like the company's ongoing portfolio ratio but expect structural performance improvements in Europe.
